content: Cancer prevention assessment includes: (1) Risk stratification—family history assessment (BRCA, Lynch syndrome identification), genetic counseling/testing criteria, risk prediction models (Gail model for breast cancer, CRC risk scores); (2) Screening eligibility—age-based guidelines (USPSTF, ACS, specialty societies), risk-based modifications, shared decision-making for borderline cases; (3) Program monitoring—screening participation rates, positivity rates, interval cancer rates, stage distribution shifts, false positive/negative rates; (4) Quality indicators—colonoscopy adenoma detection rate (>25% men, >15% women), mammography recall rates, cervical screening adequacy; (5) Population surveillance—cancer registry data (SEER), incidence/mortality trends, disparity monitoring. Emerging approaches include risk-stratified screening intervals, liquid biopsies for early detection, and polygenic risk scores. Cost-effectiveness analyses guide resource allocation decisions.
